MERCANTILE AND MONEY ARTICLE.
Monday Evening.
Messrs. Mort and Co. sold, to-day, by auction,
on the ground, at Collingwood, the following
portion of the Collingwood estate :—-Colling¬
wood House and grounds, out-offices, garden,
&c., comprising 23 acres of land, for £1350, to
Mr. Gillespie ; four building allotments, with
frontage to the Great Southern Road, at from
3s 9d. to 6s. per foot; allotments section
7 and 8, opposite Collingwood House,
to Mr. Gillespie, at 5s, per foot.
They also sold, after the sale, the Steam Flour
Mills, miller's cottage, &c., for £2000, to Mr.
Henry Moore ; the butcher's shop for £315, to
Mr. Bond. The attendance of buyers was good,
and the bidding for some lots was spirited.
After the sale several offers were made at im¬
proved prices for the allotments of land and
small farms, and no doubt we shall have to re¬
cord sales privately of a large portion of this
estate. The total amount realised to-day was
£4370 3s. 6d.
The adjourned meeting with reference to the
proposed company for the purchase of the
Alpacas, will be held to-morrow, at 2 o'clock.
The Northern Escort arrived to-day from the
Rocky River Diggings, with 1945 ounces of
gold-dust.
We append Messrs. Schmidt, Kastermann,
and Co.'s Circular, dated Penang, 24th January,
1859:
Produce -All our principal articles of exports have been in
great demand, chiefly for America, throughout the month past,
and, supplies falling short, an advance on former rates has in
many instances been submitted to.
- Tin -Large purchases for the American markets at the end of
last and beginning of this month at from 27¼ dollars to 27½ dol¬
lars per picul. The season for heavy supplies from Junk Ceylon
is now nearly over ; the market has been further influenced by
favourable accounts from India and England and the declining
rate of Exchange, and prices have gradually gone up to 28 dol¬
lars 60 cents per picul, which is the rate paid this day.
Sugar -Considerable shipments to London, per Pilgrim and
Golconda, for planters' account. Local sales for India and Singa¬
pore at 5 dollars to 74 dollars for white, 4¼ dollars to 5½ dollars
for yellow, and 3 dollars to 3½ dollars for brown.
Rum. - The Golconda takes a large quantity to England for
planters' account. Sales for the Singapore and China market at
50 to 55 cents per gallon, 30 to 35 per cent, over proof
Pepper.- There has been much competition for the few lots
coming to market between the vessels loading for America, and
prices have been forced up to 7 dollars 90 cents per picul. for West
Coast, and 7 dollars 65 cents per picul for East Coast. A small lot
of Truery of fair quality sold at 7 dollars 77½ cents per picul. The
pressing demand for America being satisfied, a decline of rates
may now be looked forward to. White pepper is scarce, and paid
as high as 14½ dollars per picul.
Onch.- None in market.
Gutta Percha.- In great demand. A small parcel has been pur¬
chased for Singapore at 23 dollars per picul.
India Rubber. - Is much wanted, and purchased as it arrives at
19½ dollars to 20 dollars per picul.
Hides - Continue in demand for America ; sales of Buffalo at
7 dollars to 7¼, and cow at 10 dollars to 10¼ per picul.
Horns. - In demand at 7 dollars to 7¼ dollars per picul. Sup¬
plies very limited.
Spices. - Nutmegs are selling at 41 dollars per picul, 110 to the
lb. Mace 34 dollars per picul. Penang cloves 23 dollars per
picul, crop very small. Zanzibar cloves 7½ dollars per picul.
Gum Benjamin.- Purchases of No 2 quality at 17 dollars to l8
dollars per picul. No supplies of No. 1.
Betelnut. - Stocks have been lightened by shipments to Cal¬
cutta ; holders are asking now 5¼ dollars per bhar, for good
quality.
Rattans.- In demand ; best Perak paid as high as 3½ dollars
per pical.
Rice.-The bulk of the new crop of Rangoon is now reaching
our market and prices have given way. We quote New Cargo at
42 dollars to 43 dollars; White Rangoon at 70 dollars, and White
Quedah at 85 dollars per enyan. Old rice is unsaleable.
Opium -Benares 793 dollars per chest, and expected to go even
higher. The last sale of Turkey was at 950 dollars per picul.
Exchanges. - Transactions have been very limited, sales are
reported at 4s. 7¼d. six months sight on London. On Calcutta
120 per cent, for short, and 213 per cent, for 30 days' sight. Com¬
pany's rupee shares and held at 210 for 100 dollars.
The following is taken from Messrs. Halliday,
Wise, and Co.'s Market Report, dated Foochow,
24th January :
Exports.- A considerable business has been done in tea during
the past fortnight, in consequence of teamen having given way in
their demands, and purchases of medium Sinchuen kinds have
been made at 1 to 2 £s. reduction on former rates.
The arrivals have again been heavy, amounting to 22,000 chests
of congou, and present stock of that description is estimated at
15,000 chests.
The settlements of the fortnight are reported at
20,000 chests of congou. 2000 chests souchong.
1,280 chests of Ningehow congou. 3400 half-chests oolong.
Our present quotations are
T. T.
Congou-common, low, and dusty .. 12 to 14 per peel., short
Good, sound, common .. 15 to 16 ditto
Common to medium, Sinchuen
kinds .. .. 17 to 21 ditto
Kaisow kinds. . .. 22 to 25 ditto;
Mangshowe .. .. 23½ to 24 ditto
Souchong - common to medium .. l6 to 24 ditto
Oolong - common to fine .. .. 15½j to 24 ditto
The only departure has been Magniperb, to London, making
the export to United Kingdom from this port 9,551,000 lbs.,
against 14,000,000 lbs., at same date last season.
The vessels on the berth are, Serrata and Ringleader, both for
London, and the former fully engaged at £3 per ton.

LAW.
SUPREME COURT.- FRIDAY.
Sittings for the TRIAL OF CAUSES.
JURY COURT.
Before Mr. Justice Dickinson and a jury of four.
HART V. MONROE.
This was an action of ejectment to recover possession
of two small cottages at Paddington.
Mr Stephen appeared for the plaintiff, and Mr.
Butler for the defendant.
The plaintiff was an elderly female named Barbara
Roper Hart and her grounds of action were very
peculiar. After having formerly lived for some six
months with the defendant, Daniel Francis Monroe,
and his wife, plaintiff had executed a conveyance
to the former, of the property in question, in conside¬
ration of his agreeing to provide her with good and
sufficient "board, lodging, and washing, for the re¬
mainder of her life." In this shape the deed was
signed and sealed, but at the foot of it there was a
memorandum, also executed by Monroe, to the effect
that if he failed at any time to execute his part of the
contract, Mrs. Hart might re-enter. The plaintiff's
case was, that she had been improperly lodged and
badly fed, but upon this point the evidence was con¬
flicting. There was, however, a question of law as well
as of fact in reference to the memorandum,
a defeasance at the foot of the deed, which has already
been referred to. This was whether the memorandum
had been executed prior to the delivery. Upon this
point the evidence was not very distinct, but it would
seem that after the execution of the conveyance, and
before handing it over to Monroe, the conveyancer,
who acted for both parties, had added it and procured
Monroe's signature, by way of rendering Mrs. Hart's
position more secure. There was a great deal of con¬
tradictory evidence as to the habits and mode of living,
&c, of the parties concerned, which need not be here
gone into.
His Honor told the jury that the conveyance was
only completed on delivery. In the defeasance was
added, about the same time that the deed was exe¬
cuted not necessarily at the same hour, or even on
the same day, but while the transaction was still
pending, and before the delivery of the conveyance
to Monroe, or to any person on his behalf - the
defeasance would be incorporated in the deed itself.
If, however, the deed had been completed and de¬
livered, the estate would have passed, and
the additional memorandum would have
no operation. If the jury found that the
defeasance had been added prior to the
delivery of the deed they would then have to find
whether or not there had been the breach com¬
plained of.
The jury found a verdict for the plaintiff.
HARPER V. JOSEPH L. MONTEFIORE.
This was an action upon a promissory note for
£160 10s,, said to have been made by the firm of
Montefiore, Murphy, and Co., in December, 1857, and
to be payable at three months from that date. The
defendant denied that the note had been made by that
firm, The plaintiff here was Mr. Josiah Harper, and
the defendant, Mr. Joseph Levi Montefiore, was sued
in the resident member of the before-mentioned firm,
which consisted, during its existence, of himself, of
Mr. Louis Murphy, and of a Mr. Edward Lawrence,
and which carried on business as brokers. The note
forming the subject matter of this action had, in point
of fact, been made by Lawrence, and the real question
at issue was whether or not it had been so made in
fraud of his partner.
Mr. Isaacs and Mr. Stephen appeared for the plain¬
tiff and the Attorney-General and Mr. Wise for the
defendant.
Lawrence had absconded from the colony in
February last. Prior to this, and while he
was still a partner, he went to plaintiff and
made a purchase of goods which he required,
as he stated, for his uncle at Geelong.
The goods having been furnished, Lawrence tendered
his own bill in payment. As, however, Lawrence
was already indebted to Mr. Harper to the extent of
some £140 - also for goods sent to his uncle at Gee¬
long - and, under the circumstances, the latter de¬
clined to accept Lawrence's own bill, insisting upon a
bill from the firm. The note at present sued upon
was then made, as well, it would appear, as
two others, amounting altogether to about £500. It
was sworn by Mr. Montefiore not only that Lawrence
had no authority to sign bills or cheques, but that,
during the time Lawrence remained a partner (about
six months) their firm never would give any nego¬
tiable paper. At the time these notes were given,
Montefiore's firm had had no transactions with plain¬
tiff, except as brokers for third persons, and were not
indebted to him. It was also stated in evidence that
plaintiff had frequently communicated with Lawrence
individually. The defence, grounded upon this
evidence, was that plaintiff must have well known he
was taking a note from Lawrence in the name of the
firm for Lawrence's own private debt.
His Honor told the jury that although a partner
would have full authority to bind the firm to which he
belonged by attaching its name to negotiable paper for
partnership purposes, he could not do this in reference
to private debts of his own. If, therefore, the jury be¬
lieved that this note was given by Lawrence for a
private debt of his own, and not for a debt of the firm,
and that plaintiff knew this to be the case, the verdict
must be for the defendant.
The jury found a verdict for the defendant.

COUNTRY WORKS
THE number of men employed on the main roads during the past
month has been 1851. The weather has retarded operations, and
the floods have done considerable damage. On the Southern
Road, all the contracts for the year have been let, and the works
will be proceeded with rapidly when the weather permits.
Stuckey's Flats new road is now passable by the mails, and the
bridges over the ravines are being proceeded with. Numerous
works in the vicinity of Wagga Wagga and Gundagai are in hand.
The piers of the bridge at Deniliquin are completed, and the
trusses, being all ready, will be very quickly put in their places,
Moulmein Bridge is finished, and tenders have been
received for Wanganella Bridge. On the Western Road,
some further contracts have been let on the Blue
Mountains, but between Hartley and Bathurst the road
has in some places got into a very bad state on account of the
contractors failing to proceed with the works. A change has been,
made by the Survey department in the site for Yale Creek bridge,
but the contractor has now been instructed to proceed with the
work on the new site. The bridge at Kite's Swamp is completed,
and that at Forbes is all but finished. On the Mudgee Road all the
works for the year are in progress, but have been retarded by the
weather. The bridges at Yellowmundi Lagoon, Cattai Creek, and
on the Blacktown Road are being proceeded with. Steps are
being taken to make good the approaches to Petersham and to
the South Creek railway station; and the works have been com¬
menced on the Long Bay and Botany Roads. In the north the
roads have suffered considerably, but with the assistance of the
West Maitland Flood Relief Committee the road between East and
West Maitland will be put in a proper state of repair ; in a short
time considerable improvements will also be undertaken on the
Morpeth Road. The large culvert at East Maitland has been
commenced, and the approaches to the Morpeth Railway station
have been undertaken by task to admit of the employment of the
destitute in the vicinity. The continued rains have caused the
almost entire failure of the light gravelled roads between Singleton
and Murrurrundi, but they are now being replaced with
metalled roads or with heavy gravelling in preparation for metal¬
ling. It has became imperative to extend several of the contracts.
The roads at Murrurrundi, though far from what they
should be, have considerably improved under Mr. Quodling's
superintendence. The works on the Black Ridges are in a very
advanced state, and would have been completed but for the bad
weather. The lowest tender for Wallabadah, or Gaspard's Gap,
being considered too high, fresh tenders have been invited ; this
will not delay the execution of the work, as during the present
weather, even if they were let, no progress could be made.
No large works in the interior have been commenced this month
under the Colonial Architect's department; but the following
buildings have been completed :-Telegraph stations at Murrurundi
and Tenterfield; a telegraph station and post-office at
Orange ; and a lock-up at Sutton Forest. A telegraph station at
Hay is on the point of completion. A commencement has been
made with the lodge at Haslem's Creek Cemetery, contracted for
by Mr. Loveridge,
The harbour works at Wollongong are being proceeded with
by Mr. Lahif, who recently took a contract for the completion of
the portion of the works that the previous contractor had left
unfinished. The masonry is being carried out by Mr. Davis, who
has set about seventy yards of stone for the sea wall.
At Kiama, little has been done at the harbour works for some
time past, the Government having three times advertised without
obtaining any eligible tenders. An offer for the completion of
the works is, believe, under consideration. In the meantime, the
excavation of the roadway at the back of the new basin is being
proceeded with.
Tenders have been called for the construction of a coal shoot,
at the Queen's Wharf, Morpeth, to be connected with the railway
The distance of the wharf from the present end of the line
is about six hundred feet. For more than half of this distance
there will be an embankment, beyond which the line will be carried
upon a timber viaduct 233 feet in length. The bays of piles will
be twenty-five feet apart ; each bay will consist of four piles
diagonally braced. The line will be on a curve of six chains
radius. Up to within a few yards of the shoot there will be a
double line ; one line will be for the loaded and the other for the
empty wagons. The first portion of the line will be an ascent,
having passed this, the loaded wagons will run down an incline of
1 in 75 and the momentum gained will take it up a short incline of
1 in 224 to the shoot, where it will be discharged ; on returning it
will be shunted off to the other line which will descend towards
the station at an incline of 1 in 150. The shoot, which will re¬
ceive the coal from the floor of the wagon, will be adjusted to
any elevation by means of winches so as to suit ships of different
draughts. The arrangements adopted at Newcastle-on-Tyne for
economising labour and facilitating the loading of vessels have
been introduced by Mr. Moriarty in preparing the designs for the
Morpeth coal shoot. Tenders are to be received on the 16th of
August, and four months will be allowed for the completion of
the work,

IMPROVEMENTS IN. SYDNEY COVE
Business people and others familiar with the process of
the steam dredge and other appliances used in connection
with deepening operations on the eastern side of Circular
Quay will, doubtless, be glad to learn that the work has
been completed. The work has been in progress about two
years. It will be remembered that the building of
three sections of new wharfs was finished about 10
or 12 months ago, two of the sections being
about 500 feet in length, and the other (the central) 300
feet. Opposite the wharf it has been necessary to secure a
depth of 29 feet at low water spring tides. For some time
past attention has been devoted to that portion of the Sydney
Cove which is bounded on the east by the central section of
the wharf. It has been necessary to blast and dredge the
rock in some places to a depth of 10 feet. The work
has not been continuous, having been carried on only
as opportunity would allow, otherwise it would, doubtless
have been completed much sooner. Serious interruption
was caused during the wool season in consequence of ships
being berthed in that part of the harbour. A corre¬
spondent, who writes above the signature of "Experi¬
enced," attributes - the delay to the character of the
machinery employed. He expresses it as his convic¬
tion that it is of an antiquated description, and
urges that if the Government had imported from
Melbourne one of Ford's Compressed Air Rock¬
borers, and other appliances of a modem character,
the work would have been expedited, and a con¬
siderable saving effected. The correspondent referred
to also thinks that the appointment of a harbour trust
would lead to the more satisfactory carrying out of
harbour improvements. We have caused inquiries to
be made about the recommendations contained in the
letter, and have learnt that it is considered that the
machinery which has been employed was the most
suitable for the purpose. The rock which had to be
removed was irregular and patchy, and consisted of a soft
kind of sandstone. Had their been a large mass of solid
rock to be operated upon, steam drills could have been
employed with advantage; but it was thought that the
"jumper" was the most suitable instrument for making
the holes required to receive the charges of dyna¬
mite with which the rock was afterwards shattered.
The dredge Samson, working two ladders, has for a con¬
siderable time past been employed dredging stiff clay and
soft rock at the approach to the new dock at Cockatoo
Island, and at that portion of Garden Island where pre¬
parations are being made for extensive wharfage for the
Imperial Navy. While not so employed the dredge has
been availed of to dredge from time to time over the blasted
rocky bottom at the new wharf on the eastern side of the
Circular Quay. Although not of large dimensions or great
power, the Samson, owing to the great length of her
ladders, is specially adapted for the work at which she
has been engaged, when a depth of over 30 feet is required.
Fitted with Napier's patent differential clutch, this dredge
is, it is said, well suited for dredging uneven rocky
bottom, a chief feature in the clutch being its adaptability
to transmit just as much power as the dredging gear may
be considered equal to without any part breaking. The
winches for working the dredge are driven from the main
engine by powerful belting, tightened by a lever under the
winchman's control, while the lowering brakes for the
ladders, being of the differential type, enable the ladder¬
man, by the application of his finger and thumb, to
lower the heavy ladders inch by inch to the work. On the
central wharf, which has been leased to the Orient Steam
Navigation Company, there has been erected a cargo shed
200 feet in length, 40 feet in width, and having a total
height of about 29 feet. The shed is open at present, but
is to be enclosed with doors. A shed at a similar descrip¬
tion is to be erected on the Messageries Maritimes Wharf,
and two others will be built upon the Phillip-street section
of the wharf, which is used for general shipping. The
whole of the work, with the exception of the dredging, has
been carried out under the supervision of Mr. Alfred Wil¬
liams, Assistant Engineer for Harbours and Rivers, and
the dredging has been executed under the direction of Mr.
A. B. Portus, Inspector of Dredges. The Orient Com¬
pany took possession of their new wharf yesterday.
The next work of importance to which the Harbours and
Rivers Department will devote its attention will be the con¬
struction of wharfs and deepening of Darling Harbour. As
there is a large mass of solid rock to be operated upon, it is
intended to employ steam drills, and inquiries are being
instituted as to the best description of machinery for this
purpose. It is intended to excavate opposite the site of the
proposed wharfs a uniform depth of 29 feet.

SOUTH AUSTRALIA.
ADELAIDE, MONDAY.
The R.M.S. Victoria sailed for Melbourne at day¬
light, and the R.M.S. Orizaba, for London, this after¬
noon.
The funeral of the late Mr. Justice Gwynne took place
to-day and was largely attended by representative men
of the colony.
One of the banks in Adelaide has given notice to its
customers that no cheques paid in will be recognised
unless certified by the bank upon which they are
drawn.
One of the 23-ton breech-loading guns for the coast
defences of this colony arrived this morning by the
Star of Greece, from London. It is intended for the
Glenelg Battery. It is an Armstrong gun, and was
specially designed for South Australia.
Dr. Haacke, formerly director of the South Aus¬
tralian Museum, and who was the naturalist of the
New Guinea Expedition under the auspices of the
Geographical Society of Australasia, has been elected a
scientific director of the Zoological Gardens at Frank¬
fort-on-the-Maine.

THE HAYMARKET HIPPODROME.
Despite the heavy downpour of rain on Saturday
night the Haymarket Hippodrome was largely at¬
tended. The Bohemian Dramatic Company produced
the American drama, "A Traitor's Doom." The stag¬
ing was good, the scenic effects being due to the
efforts of Mr. L. Casey. The principal characters
were sustained by Mr. Milton Moss, Mr. J. A. Hamil¬
ton, Mr. C. Archer, Mr. S. M'Gowan, Miss Ethel Grey,
Miss Louie Hartley, and Miss Violet Yates. "A
Traitor's Doom" will be played each evening until
Friday, when "Dick Turpin's Ride to York" will be
produced. A matinee will be held on Saturday af¬
ternoon.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
LITTLE COOGEE SHOOTING CASE.
PATIENT'S DEPLORABLE CONDITION.
The case of George Leslie Miller, who is
charged with shooting at Lily May Davis, in
June last, at Mundarrah Towers, Little Coo¬
gee, was again before the Paddington Police
Court yesterday, before Mr Aubrey Mowle,
Chamber Magistrate. The hearing was spe¬
cially arranged to enable medical testimony
to be given as the doctor who had examined
Miss Davis at the hospital was about to leave
on an extended trip.
Evidence was given to the effect that Miss
Davis, upon examination, was found to be
suffering from a punctured wound under the
right armpit. On July 5 Constable O'Brien
was handed a bullet, which had been extract¬
ed; and also two flattened pieces of bullet
which were found in her clothing. At pre¬
sent Miss Davis was suffering from paralysis
of both legs, She would not be able to ap¬
pear at court for at least four weeks, and
even then she would have to be carried.
To Mr J W Abigail (who appeared for
the accused), witness stated that it was not
possible to predict when Miss Davis would be
able to appear. There was a doubt as to
whether she would regain the use of her limbs,
which would always be paralysed. She had
an injury to the spine, which no operation
could cure.
Mr Abigail suggested, with a view to sav¬
ing Miss Davis as much suffering and incon¬
venience as possible, that he attend with the
magistrate at the hospital for the taking of
further evidence. It would save Miss Davis
being brought to the court.
Mr Mowle thanked Mr Abigail for the sug¬
gestion, and said he would endeavour to have
the suggestion carried out.
The further hearing was remanded to Sep¬
tember 29. |
The accused was in a pitiable condition, and
was obviously suffering from bad health. It
was stated he was under medical treatment
at the gaol.

A CENTENARIAN.
Active at 101.
AN EPPING RESIDENT.
Probably the oldest living woman in Aus¬
tralia is Mrs. John Jenkins, now living with
her daughters, Mrs. Beveridge, at Blairgowrie,
Epping.
MRS. JOHN JENKINS.
Mrs. Jenkins is in her 102nd year, having
been born near Campbelltown on May 18,1822,
She has thus lived through the reigns of four
or five rulers of Great Britain, and has seen
Sydney develop from a village on the banks of
the Tank Stream.
In spite of the weight of 102 years, Mrs.
Jenkins still enjoys good health, and is still
active. At her home may be seen four gene¬
rations together - mother, daughter, grand¬
daughter, and great-granddaughter. All her life
Mrs. Jenkins has been remarkably active, and
in her youth she was a very clever horse¬
woman. She spent many years on stations
near Gundagai, in which town she is very well
known. She lived on several stations with her
husband. On one occasion while she was visit¬
ing friends a gang of bushrangers attacked the
place, and in the struggle that ensued one
bushranger was slain. At other times Mrs.
Jenkins and her family narrowly escaped en¬
counters with bushrangers. Her memory and
sight are exceptionally good. She reads with¬
out the aid of glasses, and often goes for the
mails. For many years she has never missed
exercising the franchise, and her comments
on present-day affairs are keen and to the
point.

OBITUARY.
MR. ANDREW MALCOLM.
BALRANALD, Tuesday.
The death took place at a private hospital
in Melbourne on Saturday of Mr. Andrew
Malcolm, 71, one of the best-known men in
Riverina, and an old resident of Balranald and
Swan Hill districts. He was senior partner
in the firm of Messrs. A. Malcolm and Son,
auctioneers, of Balranald. When he first came
to this district he became overseer of Canally
Station, which ran 80,000 sheep. He held this
position for five years, when he became pro¬
prietor of the Riverina "Recorder," and en¬
tered into the business of stock and station
agent.
He was secretary of the Balranald Racing
Club for 45 years, a commissioner of affi¬
davits in Victoria and New South Wales,
and, until his death, was coroner. He was a
Grand Lodge officer in New South Wales in
the Masonic Lodge, and was one of the foun¬
dation members of the Balranald Masonic
Lodge. The funeral took place in the Pres¬
byterian portion of Balranald cemetery, this
morning.

CENTRAL CRIMINAL COURT.
(Before Mr. Justice Stephen.)
Mr McKean, K.C., Senior Crown Prose¬
cutor.
NOT GUILTY ON GROUND OF INSANITY.
Kathleen May Beeby, 30, was charged with
having murdered her son Jack Beeby, aged
5 years, at Wollongong, on May 9 last.
Mr McKean said that the unfortunate
woman, who was single, was found on Wol¬
longong beach after she had walked into the
sea. The child, who was strapped to her,
was dead. The accused had been in domestic
service and was in desperate circumstances.
Her child was subnormal, and it would be
submitted that at the time the tragedy occurred
accused was temporarily insane. Accused had
made a statement to the effect that she in¬
tended to drown herself and the boy.
It was stated in evidence that, accused had
done war work and had emigrated to Aus¬
tralia.
Dr Holloway, visiting surgeon at Long Bay
Penitentiary, said he had examined accused
on several occasions since May 12. In his
opinion she was normal. "Certainly she is
not insane," he added.
Nellie McKenzie Gray, matron of a home
for the destitute and betrayed, stated that
she had known accused since 1926, when she
was an inmate of the home. Accused was
passionately fond of her son and was a "won¬
derful mother."
The jury returned a verdict of not guilty
on the ground of insanity.
His Honor directed that accused be kept
in custody during the Governor's pleasure.
Mr Brian Clancy and Mr W. F. Sheahan
(instructed by Messrs Owen and Son, of Wol¬
longong, by their Sydney agents, Messrs A.
J. Taylor, William Arnott, and Co.) appeared
for the accused.
TRIAL POSTPONED.
On the application of Mr Sheahan his
Honor postponed until the next sittings of
the Court the trial of Alfred John Dillon, 21,
who, it was stated, is to be charged with hav¬
ing murdered Cecil William Bethel McCor¬
mack in William-street, city, on May 13
last. Dillon stood his trial on this charge at
the present sittings early this month, but
the jury disagreed, and Dillon was remanded
for trial.
Mr Sheahan (instructed by Messrs. R. D.
Meagher, Sproule, and Co.) appeared for
Dillon.

LAUNCH IN TROUBLE.
¬
Taken in Tow by Rowing Boat.
A large launch, in which two men were fish¬
ing broke down off Jibbon Point, on the
National Park side of Port Hacking at about
1.30 o'clock yesterday afternoon. They were
about two miles off the shore. During the
afternoon their distress signals were noticed
at Cronulla, and the police endeavoured to
have a boat sent from Sydney to rescue the
men.
A Sydney boat was not available for the
rescue work, and at about 3.30 two local
fishermen put off from Port Hacking in a
pulling-boat to row to the disabled launch.
The sea was calm, and the fishermen were
able to tow the launch to Port Hacking.
The occupants stated that the engine had
failed and that they had signalled for assist¬
ance when the launch commenced to drift
away from the shore.

NEW WORKS.
City architects yesterday reported that,
whilst general business still showed signs of
improvement, few contracts were let during
the past week. There is a considerable amount
of remodelling being done to shops and offices
within the city, and instructions for new cot¬
tages and residences in the outer suburbs,
especially in the northern districts, are being
placed in increasing numbers.
J. Aubrey Kerr, architect, has accepted the
tender of R. Pennington. Annandale, for al¬
terations to 116-118 Clarence-street, city, and
is calling tenders for a new boiler-house and
gantry at a factory in Rushcutter Bay dis¬
trict .
Fowell and McConnell have accepted the
tender of Alfred Stevens, Bowral, for additional
classrooms at the Tudor House School, Moss
Vale.
Clifford H. Finch, architect, has accepted
the tender of W. Huish, Campsie, for the
conversion of a residence in Mona-road, Dar¬
linghurst, into flats, with two garages; the
tender of H. J. Kershaw, Lindfield, for the
erection of a residence in Waimea-road,
Roseville; and the tender of J. Bunting, city,
for painting and renovations at the Church
of England Home for Boys, Carlingford. He
is preparing plans for a grandstand and
waiting-room for the Woy Woy Council.
G. S. Keesing has accepted the tender of
Robert Wall and Son for alterations to busi¬
ness premises in George-street, city.
Peddle, Thorpe, and Walker have accepted
the tender of V. Smellie, Hurstville, for the
erection of a tennis clubhouse at Rockdale for
the Illawarra Suburbs Lawn Tennis Associa¬
tion.
Plans have been approved by the Manly
Council, and tenders will shortly be called
by Emil Sodersteen, architect, for the altera¬
tions at the Manly Hotel and its increase in
height to nine storeys. It is estimated that
the additions will cost about £30,000.
B. Hadley, architect, has accepted the ten¬
der of W. Schofield for alterations and addi¬
tions to business premises, 362-64 Pitt-street,
city, and the tender of Bray and Holliday for
the shop fronts.
Sanction has been given for the erection
of a new Presbyterian Church at Corowa,
and tenders will shortly be invited for the
job.
The Government has approved of £4175
being expended on the remodelling of the
Bega District Hospital.
A new factory is being erected in Hender¬
son-road, Alexandria, by M. Bowcock, builder,
Ashfield.
A Scouts' hall is being erected in Abercorn¬
street. Bexley, by A. Eden, Belmore.
A block of flats is being erected in Victoria-
street, Lewisham, by H. Scutts, Dulwich Hill.
H. and E. Sidgreaves, Ltd., have secured
contracts for new shop fronts and alterations
to Station, House, Hurstville, for Washington
H. Soul, Pattinson and Co. (Morrow and Gor¬
don, architects); shop fronts for J. H. Marks,
Leeton, New South Wales (G. W. A. Welch,
architect); display counters and interior fit¬
ments to new premises, Liverpool and Castle¬
reagh streets, for G. J. Coles and Co., Ltd.
(Harry Norris, Melbourne, and Budden and
Mackey, Sydney, architects in association).

LEVEL CROSSING.
"DEATH TRAP TO CHILDREN."
"The level crossing at Como is a veritable
death trap to children attending the Como
school," stated the Como and District Pro¬
gress Association to the Sutherland Shire
Council.
The association asked that an overhead
bridge or subway be constructed, especially
as the population in the district was rapidly
increasing. About 80 of these children had
to use the crossing each day, and it was asked
that until improvements were made a police¬
man or porter be placed at the crossing for
an hour in the morning and afternoon.
Councillor Lawrence said children could
easily walk in front of trains, and one had
already been killed at the crossing, which was
the most dangerous in the shire.
The council decided to co-operate with the
association and make requests as outlined.

"PAGEANT OF
NATIONS."
GREAT SPECTACLE.
Magnificent Scenes.
The "Pageant of Nations," which was
presented in the Sydney Town Hall last
night, and will be staged every night
this week as part of the Anniversary
celebrations, is one of the most spec¬
tacular and elaborately produced en¬
tertainments ever seen in Australia.
Full of glittering colour, the pageant is
something of a feast both for the eye and the
ear, in its brilliant national settings,
ensembles, and dances, and in its sparkling
music.
The crowded hall was almost transformed,
with the organ blotted out, a vast, gold
pillared stage and centre platform built speci¬
ally for the occasion, And more than 900 men,
women and children in a blaze of spotlights
for a score or more of lavish scenes.
The pageant was written and produced
by Mr. J. J. Collins for the Celebrations
Council, in collaboration with the Consular
Corps. It was attended by the Governor and
Lady Wakehurst, the Chief Justice, Sir Frede¬
rick Jordan, and Lady Jordan, the Premier
and Mrs. Stevens, the Minister in Charge of
the Celebrations, Mr. Dunningham, and Mrs.
Dunningham, and other representative citizens.
JOHN BULL'S CONGRATULATIONS.
The brilliant scene opened with a prelude
in which portly John Bull (Mr. Claude Flem¬
ing), grasping the hand of young Australia
(Master Ronald Rousel), briefly addressed the
audience and expressed the pride with which
they were celebrating the 150th birthday of
one of "their sturdiest sons."
The strikingly impressive Empire pageant,
constituting the first part of the programme
and followed by the pageant and parade of
the nations, is headed by England, symbolised
by an old English inn in the background;
the Sydney Folk Song Choir, as huntsmen in
rollicking hunting songs, and by a ballet of
young women, also in hunting costume. The
scene shifts to India, with lavishly-costumed
girls, traders, beggars, and tourists in a bazaar.
With his entourage in glittering robes and
turbans, an Indian prince makes a majestic
entry. Canada next comes into the sparkling
picture. Members of the Langlea Operatic
Society, in the uniforms of the Canadian
Royal North-west Mounted Police, sing a
spirited chorus as they march down the centre
platform and join Totem Pole girls and Red
Indians, and Rose Marie (Miss Gwladys
Evans).
IRELAND AND WALES.
Ireland is next brilliantly portrayed, in an
enchanting assemblage of colleens and
harpists, the scene closing with a joyous Irish
jig. Then comes Wales, with men and
women in picturesque national costumes, and
folk songs by the Sydney Folk Song Choir.
There is another storm of applause as a
sturdy Maori chieftain conveys the greetings
of New Zealand and Samoa, and is followed
by Maori men and girls, in posture dances
and other movements.
Following New Zealand comes South Africa.
Through a huge portrait of Cecil Rhodes is
seen faintly, first, a university quadrangle,
symbolising Rhodes's great work in the cause
of education, and then a diamond mine.
Miss South Africa closes a brilliant scene
as she moves up the steps and unfolds a
vast black train, shimmering with diamonds.
Scotland next comes into the scene with
the merry skirl of pipes, the joyous swing of
kilts, and spirited Scottish dancing and
marching.
EMPIRE PARADE.
Even more spectacular and impressive is
the closing Empire parade, in which 25 or
more countries are represented, in period
settings and traditional costumes, and headed
by standard-bearers. The last to make it
entry is Australia, symbolised strikingly by
picturesquely-costumed characters, represent¬
ing gum nuts, koalas, waratahs, wattles, and
flannel flowers. Following Australia, and
closing the stately procession, is Britannia.
The vast stage is a blaze of colour as
Britannia takes the centre of it, and is
joined by Young Australia on her right and
John Bull on her left, to the inspiring and
stirring notes of "Land of Hope and Glory."
PARADE OF NATIONS.
The nations of the world are represented
in magnificent scenes featuring their national
music and dances. With the whole of them
finally grouped, the curtains rise to reveal
also the British Empire. On the entry of
Young Australia, there is a burst of joyous
music and white doves soaring above as the
figure of Peace appears.
The choir, conducted by Mr. Horace Keats,
also plays a distinctive part in the pageant.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
JUNIOR HOCKEY.
Australian Championship.
QUEENSLAND AND N.S.W. WIN
The junior interstate hockey championship car¬
nival was begun at North Brighton yesterday. New
South Wales, Victoria, South Australia and Queens¬
land fielded teams, all the players being under the
age of 21 years.
In the two matches played yesterday, the form
displayed was of a high standard. Although Vic¬
toria and South Australia were beaten decisively
by New South Wales and Queensland respectively,
their teams fought strenuously. The South Aus¬
tralians, making their first appearance in a junior
championship lacked experience, particularly in at¬
tack. Smith, Victoria's goalkeeper, amazed with
his saves against New South Wales. He appears
a custodian with bright prospects of international
honours.
New South Wales beat Victoria by 6 goals to
nil. Smith early stopped a short corner in the
match, kicking the ball clear. He saved again with a
hard kick when Ryan took the ball into the circle
after combining well with Davis and Leslie. The
country members of the New South Wales team
speedily adapted themselves to the style of play of
the Sydney men. Lucas, of Lismore,opened the
scoring after receiving a straight, slow hit from
Davis. Although hard pressed, Victoria stood up
well but could not finish off its movements. New
South Wales scored its second goal after a penalty
fully taken by Davis with Smith fell on the ball.
Smith obstructed Davis in attempting to clear the
ball, and a penalty goal was awarded.
Stevens increased New South Wales's goals to
three by showing splendid anticipation, following a
short corner hit by Lucas into the goal mouth.
New South Wales continued its pressure in the
second half and Dalby scored after receiving a
pass from Lucas. Victoria then improved consid¬
erably, and for 10 minutes kept up a constant
attack, but its efforts were fruitless. Lucas scored
New South Wales's fifth goal after a passing rush
by Davis. Stevens and Ryan and Dalby netted
the sixth after a centre by Leslie from the right
wing.
Queensland beat South Australia by 6 goals to
1. Harrold scored four goals and Siggs and Ren¬
gart one each for Queensland. Jones was South
Australia's scorer.
TO-DAY'S MATCHES
The championship will he continued to-day Vic¬
toria will play Queensland at 2 p.m., and New South
Wales will meet South Australia at 3.30.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
DRAFTING
WOOL PLANS
CANBERRA, Friday. - Decisions
on wool marketing after the war
may be made at meeting of full
Federal Cabinet on Monday
week.
A Cabinet sub-committee including
the Prime Minister, Mr. Chifley, the
Minister for Post-war Reconstruction,
Mr. Dedman, and if available, the
Minister for Commerce and Agricul¬
ture, Mr. Scully, will have a talk next
Monday with members of the Aus¬
tralian delegation to the recent wool
conference in London. It is believed
that a draft agreement was drawn up
by the London conference and will be
presented to the Government at this
talk.
Observers think that any agreement
which is to be acceptable to Australia
must provide for some marketing
organisation to handle wool stocks
built up during the war, on lines
rather similar to those of Bawra at
the end of the war of 1914-18.
Particular interest in the future in¬
tentions of the British Government
will probably revolve round the alter¬
native question of reversion to a free
wool auction market or of continua¬
tion of the appraisement system which
has applied during the war.

Archbold Estate To Get
Sewer: Council Protest
The Water Board has
decided to sewer the
Archbold Estate at Rose¬
ville immediately.
Kuring-gai Municipal Coun¬
cil last night received a letter
from the board announcing
this decision.
The council then passed a
resolution expressing surprise
that other and older built-up
areas were not given prefer¬
ence over the Archbold
Estate.
[In the Legislative Assembly
last month the Leader of the
Opposition, Mr. V. H. Treatt,
said the Archbold Estate had
been resumed by the Government
for more than £20,000. He
said that of the 31 people who
had been allotted homes on the
estate without ballot 11 were
Public servants.]
While Kuring-gai Council
was discussing the Water
Board's letter Alderman E.
Solomon said other areas in
the municipality had been
developed for years without
any hint of their being
sewered.
"We now find the Archbold
Estate is getting preference
over all other areas in the dis¬
trict which have waited for
years for sewerage," he said.
Alderman A. Campbell,
representative for the Water
Board, said: "This is called a
short extension and it is not
a priority. It is the policy of
the Water Board to do short
extensions. The Housing Com¬
mission doesn't get priorities."
Ald. Solomon: Some people
have waited 15 years for the
sewer.
Ald. Campbell: The Housing
Commission is not getting a
priority.
Alter the meeting Alderman
Solomon said: "There is no
doubt that pressure has been
brought to bear on the Water
Board. I think the Minister,
or if not the Minister then
the Housing Commission, has
used some pressure. There
are only five or six houses
already built on the estate and
only 26 to be built."

of New Zealand.

EXPLOSION IN FIRE¬
PLACE.
SUPPOSED DYNAMITE IN
FIREWOOD.
ARMIDALE, Tuesday.
While a lad named Aubrey Cowley was sit¬
ting reading in the dining-room of his resi¬
dence at North Armidale a couple of nights
ago, a sudden explosion occurred in the fire¬
place, blowing the lad across the room, the
contents of the fireplace following, setting
fire to the room. The lad was found to have
a gash in his thigh, and his face and hands
were burned. The flames were extinguished,
and the boy was removed to the hospital
suffering from serious injuries. The book he
was reading saved the lad's eyes from being
blown out. The wood on the fire had been
lying in his father's yard for some months.
It was evident that a plug of dynamite had
been placed in the wood.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
HINTS OF FASHION.
- Linen suits are principally cut with a
skirt and polonaise, the latter generally
the Marguerite.
- All the new over-skirts are made
long - very long at the back, but draped
high at the sides, with the apron front.
- On black satin striped grenadine bias
ruffles may be used as trimmings; on
striped organdy bias ruffles or bias bands,
edged with lace.
- New tinted scarfs for street wear are
composed of twilled silk, with wide dia¬
gonal stripes, shaded from dark to light in
the same color.
- Fine embroidery wrought in the
linen is now the fashionable method of or¬
namenting underclothing, and is much
more suitable as well as more durable
than lace or ruffling.
- Neck ties or bows are of pale twilled
silk, with or without fringed ends, the
fringe forming knotted tassels. Others
are of twilled silk edged with Valen¬
ciennes, the ends forming a cascade com¬
posed of alternate shades of silk and lace.
- As the warm weather advances many
different styles for suits will be introduced,
but the skirt and the polonaise will be
mainly adhered to, and the simpler the
design for washing materials the better it
looks, and the longer it retains its fresh
appearance.
- Satin striped organdy is a novelty in
white muslin; the stripes are an inch
broad and have a satin finished surface.
It "does up" nicely and will make pretty
summer suits or house dresses, and it will
need but little trimming. Black satin-
striped grenadine is used this season in¬
stead of the black with a white stripe,
which was so much in vogue last year.
Of course both will be worn, but the all
black is later and considered more lady¬
like. GLEANINGS.
- It is reported that there are seventy-
five counties in Texas in which there is
not a single Sunday school.
- Widow Fay of Oregon has set up a
cigar manufactory, and her weeds are said
to be the best in the state.
- Converted Indians sing "Now I Lay
Me" with warwhoop accompaniment.
The effect is said to be very soothing.
- The Japanese make a strong string of
paper, and we have seen a very good cord
of wood, but not lately. - [Boston Trans.
- A correspondent from Salt Lake City
says the Mormon girls have the freshest
fairest, clearest, and most healthy comp¬
lexions he ever saw.
- Within the last ten years 160,000,000
acres of public lands have been given away,
not, however, to the landless, homeless,
the hymonial platform, adjusted the fatal
noose, and swung off into that unsilent
bourne whence he can never return, save
by the Indianapolis connecting lines."
- An embryo Barnum of nine years, in
Virginia City, Nevada, painted his little,
brother in the latest style of the fierce
Sioux, and exhibited him as a captured
son of Spotted Tail, at twenty-five cents a
ticket. His mother attended the show
and recognised and claimed her darling
son.
- For several months past W. H. Allen,
M. D., has been living in Bloomington,
healing the blind and afflicted, occupying
rooms in the People's bank block. He
came to a climax on Tuesday night by
eloping with the wife of a German named
Bomgardner, who lives somewhere near
the "Forty Acres."
- The scientific men who have been
watching Mount Vesuvius for the past
month declare that there are unmistaka¬
ble indications that a spout will occur
within another month. The mountain is
groaning and sighing, clouds of ashes are
being blown out, and a warning has been
given near inhabitants to gather their
household effects and remove to safer
places.
- A good story is told of a St. Louis
nobby dry goods clerk who attended a
dance in the rural district a few eve¬
nings since. He wore a Cheviot shirt, and
put on a great many airs. He was some¬
what taken down, however, when he over¬
heard one country lass say to another:
"That St Louis chap slings on a heap of
style for a feller that wears a bed-tick
shirt."

THE LADIES OF THE CANNIBAL
ISLES.
On our first page will be found an
article extracted from the Illustrat¬
ed American under the above cap¬
tion. The name of the author is
John C. Bowker, M. D.
It is a tissue of indecencies, un¬
truths and vulgarity as ugly and un¬
euphonious as the name of the
author himself. In all probability,
if the idiot or fool who penned the
lines ever visited this country, he
associated himself solely with the
low-down class, whose only enjoy¬
ment in life is to live in the slums
of the old Cape Horn and the mo¬
dern Japan Sea. He certainly never
could have met with decent society
here, or if he did, having bummed
off them to his fullest extent he
turned around and libels them. It
is this class of vipers who do so
much to injure the fair fame of the
Hawaiian race and their women.
Strangely enough when such emi¬
nent writers as Mark Twain, Charles
Warren Stoddard, even Joaquin
Miller, and Robert Louis Stevenson,
write about us they dip their pens
into mellifluous truth and not into
putrid gall.
"Bowker" probably never knew
that nearly all the leading families
are intermarried with Hawaiians, or
that the strongest, handsomest and
best of the modern race to be found
in the schools and colleges, and in
the highest position socially, commercially
and in Government posi¬
tions are either Hawaiians or half¬
castes, and that the race - morally,
physically and educationally - is
progressing and not retrograding.
His last paragraph may be true
in regard to a limited number of
women, who are, or ought to be, on
the registered list for special pur¬
poses, but happily for us who live
here and intermarry with them and
raise children, we find it to be abso¬
lutely and maliciously untrue. Our
census returns prove the fact that
Hawaiian women are good child¬
bearers, and in the country apart
from seaports and our garrison
towns especially prolific.
Admitted that during the past
few years, in Honolulu, there has
been a largely increased mortality
among Hawaiian infants of the poor¬
er classes, that is not the fault of
the Hawaiian mothers, but of the
unfortunate conditions of a want of
proper system of drainage and bad
water, and the poverty inflicted upon
the natives through land sharks and
thieves and political persecutions.
No! "Bowker" our Hawaiian girls
and women, the mothers of the
future, are as healthy, strong and
beautiful as any you can produce
against them; and in tastefulness of
dress, in gracefulness of deport¬
ment, in refinement of manners, and
in brain capacity can discount the
women of many a more ancient
"civilized" country. In the reward
of the survival of the fittest our
Hawaiian girls will succeed in cap¬
turing the prize, and our present
and future generations, when Ha¬
waii's autonomy is an assured fact,
will be the pride of the world.

The New Ambassador to France.
Gen. Horace Porter, the new Am¬
bassador to France, is the author of
the most complete and comprehen¬
sive history of Grant's military
career now appearing in The Cen¬
tury Magazine under the title of
"Campaigning with Grant." Wash¬
ington is very happy in her repre¬
sentative at the principal courts of
Europe. Gen. Porter, a worthy suc-
cessor of Mr. Whitelaw Reid, is a
man of wit, a scholar, a soldier, and
a gentleman. It was he who said at
a Fourth of July celebration, "Gen¬
tlemen, let us not forget that we
were all Englishmen until we signed
the Declaration of Independence."
And on another occasion, when the
evacuation of Yorktown was men¬
tioned in a half apologetic way by a
speaker at a banquet where a com¬
pany of English gentlemen were
present, I heard him in his pleasant,
humorous way remark, "Anybody
who knows Yorktown couldn't be
surprised at the English general
being quite willing to get out."
With a native American's love for
his country Gen. Porter has a hearty
admiration for this other land of
liberty which has set the example of
freedom to the whole world. - Joseph
Hatton in Cigarette Papers.

ROBERTS GRATEFUL
FOR QUEEN'S WORDS
LONDON, Feb. 28. - In her dispatch to
Lord Roberts following the announcement
of the surrender of General Cronje her
Majesty said:
"Accept for yourself and all under your
command my warmest congratulations on
this splendid news."
Lord Roberts replied as follows:
"All under my command are deeply
grateful for your Majesty's most gracious
message. Congratulations from their
Queen are an honor the soldiers dearly
prize."
General Buller has wired his thanks to
the Queen for her telegram of gracious
sympathy and encouragement.
CONGRESSMEN TO CRONJE.
WASHINGTON, Feb. 28.— Representa¬
tive Fitzgerald of Massachusetts to-day
passed around among his Democratic col¬
leagues the following cable to General
Cronje, the defeated Boer general. Up to
to-day, about thirty of them had signed it:
"General Cronje, Cape Town, Africa:
Members of the United States House of
Representatives congratulate you and
your soldiers on the magnificent display
of courage and heroism in your brave
fight for human rights."
CONFLICTS AT BELFAST.
BELFAST, Feb. 28. - A rumor, that
Ladysmith had been relieved led to street
demonstrations in Belfast to-day which
precipitated conflicts between the Orange¬
men and Nationalists. There was consid¬
erable stone throwing and many persons
were injured and some property damaged.
BOERS SAID TO USE
EXPLOSIVE BULLETS
ROBERTS IMPRESSED
BY CRONJE'S INGENUITY
LONDON, March 1. - Winston Churchill,
in a dispatch from Colenso dated Tuesday,
says:
"The condition of the wounded who were
untended on the hillside Sunday was so
painful that General Buller sent a flag of
truce to the enemy and it was arranged
that throughout Sunday military move¬
ments should continue on both sides, but
there should be no shooting. This truce
terminated at dusk. The Boers then re¬
sumed a furious musketry attack on the
British left. The attack was repulsed.
Fighting continues vigorously. We shall
see who can stand 'bucketing' best, the
Briton or the Boer."
Mr. Churchill goes on to say that there
is abundant proof of the Boers using a
larger proportion of illegal bullets, no
fewer than five different kinds of explod¬
ing or expanding bullets having been
found. He also asserts that the Boers are
employing armed Kaffirs, and he adds:
"I have always tried to be fair toward
the Boers, but after making every allow¬
ance it must be said that they show when
in stress a very dark, cruel and vengeful
underside of character."
FOOD IS YET SCARCE.
KIMBERLEY, Feb. 28. - Colonel Peak¬
man, with a mounted force and a Maxim,
proceeded to Barkley West, where he was
warmly welcomed. He left a strong guard,
and then proceeded to Longlands. Wind¬
sortown and Klopdam. There were ru¬
mors of Boers in the neighborhood, but no
demonstration occurred.
The Diamondfield Advertiser draws
pointed attention to the fact that al¬
though Kimberley was relieved about two
weeks ago, there has been no amelioration
in regard to the food supply. It is still
impossible, as it was during the invest¬
ment, to procure a tin of condensed milk
or cocoa without a medical certificate.
The inhabitants continue without many
of the common articles of food, although
meat rations have been increased to half
a pound.

That mining operations were carried
out in this country by the Romans during
their occupancy there is abundance of ev¬
idence, but nothing in this direction has
equalled the discoveries recently made in
connection with the St. Domingo mines
of Spain. The were worked by the Ro¬
mans in what may be termed a scientific
manner considering their appliances for
in some of them they actually excavated
galleries for draining purposes nearly
three miles in length, whilst in others the
water was raised by means of wheels
carrying it over rocks. Eight of these
have recently been found by the miners
who are employed in contiguous work¬
ings in these old mines and all in a high
state of preservation. They are made of
wood, the wheels and axles, where the
greatest strain would be, being of oak and
the other parts of pine. It is estimated
that the wheels are at least 1,500 years
old, although the wood is in a perfect
state of preservation owing to the water
being discharged with metallic salts, in¬
cluding those of copper and iron. It
would appear that the wheels must have
been worked by men on the treadmill
principle by standing upon one side of it,
the water being raised by one wheel into
a large basin, and then lifted another
stage by a second one, and then in the
same way by the others. Not the least
interesting matter in connection with the
discovery is the state in which the wood
was found. When wood is placed in wa¬
ter or near to it, the decay is rather rap¬
id, but there are means by which it can
be kept for ages in a good state of preser¬
vation, as shown in the case of the St.
Domingo mines. Some metallic refuse is
found even now sufficient for the purpose.
Kyanising has long been in use for pre¬
venting fermentation and decay, which is
done by steeping the timber in corro¬
sive sublimate, the perchloride of mercu¬
ry. Creosole has also been found an ex¬
cellent preservative of timber subjected
to the action of water. It may also be
said that whilst the Romans knew how
to preserve timber underground, the
Greeks also knew how to keep it in a
good state in their buildings. This they
did by charring the timber before placing
it in position, so that we are told "the
beams of the theater at Herculaneum
were converted into charcoal by the burn¬
ing lava which overflowed the city, and
after a lapse of more than 1,900 years
were found as entire as if they had been
formed but yesterday." The same prop¬
erty was known even before this, as the
famous temple of Ephesus was built on
piles charred to prevent them from decay.
The history of the past - even in mining
it appears after a lapse of 1,000 years -
may be, of advantage to the miners of
the present age. - London Mining Jour¬
nal.

SPARROWS ATTACK MEN;
MAKE THEM QUIT WORK
DANVILLE Pa., June 21. - Sparrows
pecking at the faces and eyes of
workmen who were trying to repair
the slate roof on the tower of Ma¬
honing Presbyterian Church, forced
them to descend and give up the job.
Hundreds of the birds had their
nests in the old tower, and sulphur
was being used to exterminate them
when they swooped down on the
men. Trustees of the church are
considering offering a reward for
successful method of getting rid of
the pests.
32 DEAD, 72 HURT IN
GERMAN MINE DISASTER
BERLIN, June 21. - Thirty-two
miners are dead, fourteen are missing
and seventy-two are suffering from
gas poisoning as the result of a dis¬
aster in the Stinnes coal mine near
Herne, today.

AIR FORCES
SEND SUB
TO BOTTOM
WITH BOMB
Explosive Dropped 2,000 Feet
By Fleet of Navy Aeroplanes
Destroys Target.
LANGLEY FIELD, HAMPTON.
VA., June 21. - At 10:40, just as the
U-117 had been sunk, a small S-E3
scout plane fell. The destroyer
Shawmut went to the assistance of
the pilot, who was Colonel Culver,
officer in charge of communications
at Langley Field. It has not been
learned here yet whether or not he
was hurt.
By W. S. JEWETT.
Times Staff Correspondent.
ABOARD AIRPLANE OFF AT¬
LANTIC COAST (by wireless to
Langley Field, Va.), June 21. - The
former German submarine U-117 was
sunk in five minutes this morning
by bombardment from the fourth
naval division, fifty miles east of
Cape Charles lightship.
The fourth division was ordered
to attack at 10:34 by wireless
from the naval air commander
aboard the U. S. S. Shawmut.
Within a minute a shower of
bombs from the N-C planes de¬
scended on the gray back of the
submersible lying awash 2,000
feet below. The ship rocked with
the concussion as the bombs took
effect, gave a convulsive shudder,
which brought her bow well above
the water, and then slowly sank
from sight - before the whirling
planes could return to the attack.
GEN. MITCHELL IN AIR.
Long before the navy planes ap¬
peared on the scene two big de
Haviland planes, one bearing Gen¬
eral Mitchell, assistant chief of the
air service, and the other a pho¬
tographer plane from Langley Field,
whirred overhead. They were pre¬
ceded by three army dirigibles,
which floated in the sky like huge
silver perfectos, poking their blunt
noses inquisitively about.
Beautiful weather accompanied the
flyers on the trip. On the way to
the objective the sea spread out like
a huge glass plain below. The first
thrill of the bombing came near
Sewall Point, where the flyers, 5,000
feet high, looked down and saw one
of the "Nancy" navy planes in
flames.
A commercial ship not far away
spied her and sped to her assistance,
according to radio dispatches reach¬
ing the pines above, all the flyers
on the burning plane were taken off
safely.
The first naval division left the
Yorkton base shortly after 9 o'clock.
It was followed by the Fourth and
Fifth division at twenty minute in¬
tervals.
NAVY CHEATS ARMY.
The entire Atlantic fleet was clust¬
ered about the submarine target to
watch the bombarding. On the way
out across the sea seven destroyers
were strung at intervals to lead
assistance in case of a forced land¬
ing. Huge superdreadnaughts swung
lazily three miles away, while de¬
stroyers bobbed easily closer to the
target.
The quick work of the navy flyers
cheated the army of a chance to dis¬
play the ability of its bombadiers.
Back at Langley Field, fifteen huge
Martin bombers were on the flying
line with humming engines waiting
the word to speed to sea if naval
flyers were unsuccessful in their at¬
tack.
Even the most sanguine expectations
of the army flyers were surpassed at
the ease with which the U-boat suc¬
cumbed. Rivalry between the army
and navy forces over the outcome of
the bombing was intense.
